title: Rewilding: What It Is and Why It Matters
body:
Rewilding is an approach to conservation that differs fundamentally from traditional wildlife management. Where traditional conservation typically involves active management to maintain particular species compositions and ecosystem states, rewilding involves withdrawing human management and allowing ecological processes to determine the outcome — trusting nature to find its own equilibrium.

The concept rests on the recognition that many of the ecosystems we consider 'natural' are in fact profoundly impoverished compared to what they once were. European landscapes that appear diverse and rich are, from a deeper ecological perspective, the remnants of systems from which large predators, megafauna, and complex trophic interactions have been absent for centuries or millennia. What we perceive as natural is the degraded baseline.

The reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone National Park in 1995 provided the most celebrated demonstration of rewilding's potential. The wolves did not merely reduce elk numbers — they changed elk behaviour, causing them to avoid valleys and gorges where they were vulnerable to predation. This behavioural shift allowed vegetation to recover in those areas, which stabilised riverbanks, which altered water flow and created habitat for beavers, fish, and birds. The cascade of effects — what ecologists call a trophic cascade — transformed the entire ecosystem. The wolves changed the rivers.

European rewilding projects, from the Knepp Estate in West Sussex to large-scale efforts in the Netherlands and Romania, have demonstrated similarly striking recoveries. Areas of degraded agricultural land, once depopulated of their native fauna and managed for monoculture production, have been transformed within decades into biodiverse ecosystems that support hundreds of species not previously present.

title: The Soil Beneath Our Feet: Why It Matters More Than Anything
body:
Soil is not dirt. The distinction is not merely semantic: dirt is inert material, while healthy soil is among the most complex and biologically rich environments on Earth. A teaspoon of healthy forest soil contains more microorganisms — bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes, and others — than there are people on Earth. These organisms form networks of astonishing complexity, converting organic matter into plant-available nutrients, suppressing pathogens, regulating water movement, and sequestering carbon.

The formation of topsoil is extraordinarily slow: approximately one centimetre per century under natural conditions. The destruction of topsoil by industrial agriculture — through tillage that destroys fungal networks, pesticide application that reduces microbial diversity, and the removal of organic matter that maintains soil structure — occurs on a timescale orders of magnitude faster. The UN Food and Agriculture Organisation has estimated that at current rates of soil degradation, the world has approximately sixty years of viable topsoil remaining.

The implications are not abstract. All terrestrial food production ultimately depends on topsoil. The degradation of agricultural soil reduces yields, increases the dependence on synthetic fertilisers (which address the symptom but worsen the underlying biological depletion), and contributes to erosion that strips topsoil into waterways, causing the algal blooms and aquatic dead zones that are increasingly common in agricultural regions worldwide.

Regenerative agriculture — an umbrella term for farming practices that build rather than deplete soil health — represents the most serious current attempt to address this crisis. The core practices include minimal tillage, cover cropping, composting, and managed grazing — all of which feed the soil microbiome and build the organic matter that distinguishes healthy soil from dirt.

title: Migratory Species and the Invisible Web Connecting Continents
body:
Migration is one of the most astonishing phenomena in the living world. Humpback whales travel up to 16,000 kilometres annually between their feeding grounds in polar waters and their breeding grounds in tropical seas. Arctic terns make a round trip of approximately 70,000 kilometres each year, travelling from the Arctic to the Antarctic and back — experiencing more daylight than any other creature on Earth. Bar-tailed godwits fly non-stop from Alaska to New Zealand, a journey of over 11,000 kilometres without food, water, or rest, using roughly half their body weight in fuel.

These journeys are not merely spectacular. They perform ecological functions that connect ecosystems across continents and hemispheres in ways that are often invisible until they fail. Migratory fish carry marine nutrients into freshwater systems when they spawn, feeding the forest ecosystems that line river banks with the decomposition of their bodies. Migratory birds consume insects in their breeding grounds (regulating pest populations) and disperse seeds in their wintering grounds (influencing vegetation). The movements of large migratory mammals transport nutrients across landscapes in their dung and their bodies.

The decline of migratory species — which is occurring at rates significantly faster than non-migratory species — removes these ecological connections. When a migratory species is lost, the consequences ripple through all the ecosystems it connected, in ways that are often not predicted until after the loss has occurred. The restoration of migratory populations is correspondingly difficult: it requires not just protecting one habitat but protecting the entire network of habitats the species uses along its migration route — a political and logistical challenge of enormous complexity.

title: Fire Ecology: Why Some Ecosystems Need to Burn
body:
Fire is not destruction. In many ecosystems — grasslands, savannas, chaparral, certain types of forest — fire is a fundamental ecological process, as necessary to ecosystem health as rainfall or predation. The species that inhabit these ecosystems have co-evolved with fire over millions of years, developing adaptations that not only tolerate fire but depend on it: seeds that germinate only after exposure to smoke or heat, bark thick enough to protect the living layer beneath from moderate fires, the ability to resprout rapidly from root systems after the above-ground structure has been burned.

Fire suppression — the policy of preventing and extinguishing all wildfires that dominated land management in North America and Australia through much of the twentieth century — is now understood to have created the conditions for the catastrophic wildfires of the early twenty-first century. By preventing the low-intensity fires that naturally cleared accumulated fuel loads, decades of suppression allowed those fuels to build to levels that produce fire behaviour unprecedented in the historical record — hotter, faster, and more destructive than the fires that the ecosystems are adapted to survive.

Aboriginal Australian fire management — the practice of prescribed burning at carefully chosen times and in specific patterns to manage fuel loads and maintain mosaic landscapes — was suppressed along with Indigenous culture by colonial governments and has only recently been recognised as a sophisticated ecological management system with demonstrably better outcomes than European fire suppression approaches.

The lesson is broadly applicable: interventions in complex ecosystems that remove natural processes — however well-intentioned — tend to produce long-term outcomes worse than those they sought to prevent. Working with ecological processes, rather than against them, is consistently the more effective strategy.

title: Pollinators in Crisis: What We Lose When Bees Disappear
body:
Approximately three-quarters of flowering plant species depend on animal pollinators — bees, butterflies, moths, flies, beetles, birds, and bats — for reproduction. Approximately one-third of the human food supply depends on pollinator-dependent crops. This is not a metaphor for ecological connection; it is a material fact with direct implications for food security, agricultural economics, and the structure of ecosystems.

Pollinator populations — particularly bees — have declined dramatically over the past several decades in most of the world's agricultural regions. The causes are multiple and interacting: habitat loss (the elimination of the wildflower-rich field margins, hedgerows, and meadows that provide nesting sites and non-crop food sources), pesticide exposure (particularly systemic neonicotinoids, which affect bee navigation and reproduction even at sublethal doses), disease (the Varroa mite, which parasitises honeybees and vectors viruses, has devastated managed bee populations globally), and climate change (which is altering the synchrony between bee activity periods and plant flowering times).

The consequences of continued decline extend well beyond honeybee-pollinated crops. Wild bees — bumblebees and the roughly 250 solitary bee species in the UK alone — are often more effective pollinators of many crops than honeybees, and they pollinate the wild plants that form the basis of broader ecological food webs. The loss of wild bee diversity has cascading effects through the entire ecosystem.

Effective responses require action at multiple scales: farm-level changes to pesticide use and habitat provision, landscape-scale connectivity of wild habitats, and policy frameworks that make pollinator-friendly practices economically competitive with current agricultural norms. Individual garden management — planting pollinator-friendly species, avoiding pesticide use, providing nesting habitat — makes a genuine contribution when multiplied across millions of households.

title: The Ocean's Role in Climate: What the Sea Does for the Planet
body:
The ocean covers approximately 71% of the Earth's surface and plays a role in the climate system so fundamental that the planet would be uninhabitable without it. Understanding what the ocean does for the climate — and what the disruption of its systems means — is essential context for understanding the full scope of climate change.

The ocean absorbs heat: it has absorbed approximately 90% of the excess heat generated by greenhouse gas emissions since industrialisation. This has significantly buffered the rate of atmospheric warming — the surface air temperature rise that has been observed would be dramatically larger without this oceanic heat absorption. But the ocean is not an infinite heat sink: its warming is itself producing consequences, including thermal expansion (which contributes to sea level rise), changes in ocean circulation, and the bleaching of coral reefs (which are extremely sensitive to water temperature).

The ocean absorbs carbon dioxide: approximately 25-30% of anthropogenic CO2 emissions are absorbed by the ocean. This is carbon that would otherwise remain in the atmosphere and contribute to warming. But CO2 dissolving in seawater forms carbonic acid, reducing the ocean's pH — a process called ocean acidification. The pH of ocean surface water has decreased by approximately 0.1 pH units since the pre-industrial period, representing a 26% increase in acidity. This acidification threatens organisms that build calcium carbonate shells and skeletons, including corals, oysters, sea urchins, and the plankton species that form the base of most marine food webs.

The ocean drives weather: the global ocean circulation system — the thermohaline circulation, or 'ocean conveyor belt' — distributes heat around the planet, making the climate of Western Europe significantly warmer than it would otherwise be at the same latitude. The freshwater influx from melting ice sheets is slowing this circulation, with uncertain but potentially dramatic consequences for regional climates.

title: The Wood Wide Web: How Trees Communicate
body:
One of the most remarkable discoveries in ecology over the past thirty years is the existence of extensive fungal networks connecting the root systems of trees in forests — networks through which trees exchange carbon, water, nutrients, and what appear to be chemical signals in ways that fundamentally revise our understanding of what a forest is. The mycorrhizal networks (from the Greek mykes, fungus, and rhiza, root) that form these connections are ancient: the fossil record shows mycorrhizal associations going back over 400 million years, predating the colonisation of land by plants. The relationship appears to be genuinely mutualistic: fungi extend the effective root surface of trees by orders of magnitude, accessing water and nutrients that root hairs cannot reach, while trees supply the fungi with carbon fixed through photosynthesis.

Suzanne Simard's research in British Columbia forests, beginning in the 1990s, demonstrated that carbon moves through mycorrhizal networks from more to less productive trees — from large, well-established trees to seedlings growing in their shade — in quantities that appear to benefit the receiving plants. Simard's concept of 'mother trees' — large, well-connected trees that act as hubs in the network — has captured popular imagination, though the degree to which these networks constitute anything like intentional communication or kin recognition remains scientifically contested.

What is clear, and what is ecologically significant, is that forests are not collections of individual trees competing for resources but systems in which individual trees are connected through shared infrastructure that moves resources in ways that benefit the system as a whole. This understanding has direct implications for forestry practice: clear-cutting not only removes trees but destroys the mycorrhizal networks that make regeneration possible, which is one reason why clear-cut forests recover more slowly and less completely than selectively harvested ones.

title: The Ocean's Role in Climate: What We Need to Know
body:
The ocean is the climate system's great regulator — its enormous thermal mass absorbs and redistributes heat from the atmosphere, its circulation patterns move that heat around the planet, and its biological processes draw down carbon dioxide from the atmosphere at scales that dwarf any human intervention yet devised. The ocean has absorbed approximately ninety percent of the excess heat generated by anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions since the industrial revolution, a service to the climate system of incalculable value — and one that is producing consequences of its own that are only beginning to be understood.

Ocean warming is changing the physical and biological properties of the ocean in ways that have cascading consequences. Warmer water holds less oxygen, and the deoxygenation of the ocean's intermediate and deep layers is already measurable and is reducing the habitable space for many marine species. Warming is also driving the poleward migration of fish species that fishing communities and ecosystems have been adapted to for generations. The bleaching of coral reefs — the expulsion of the symbiotic algae that give corals their colour and provide the energy that sustains reef ecosystems — has become a global crisis at current temperatures, and the frequency and severity of bleaching events will only increase with further warming.

Ocean acidification — the consequence of the ocean's absorption of carbon dioxide, which reacts with seawater to form carbonic acid — is the other major chemical change occurring in parallel. The ocean's pH has already decreased by approximately 0.1 units since the industrial revolution, representing a thirty percent increase in hydrogen ion concentration. This change threatens the ability of shell-forming organisms — pteropods, oysters, corals, many plankton species — to build and maintain their calcium carbonate structures, with implications that cascade up food webs that ultimately include humans.

title: Rewilding: What It Is and Why It Matters
body:
Rewilding — the restoration of natural processes and, where possible, the species that once maintained them — is among the most ambitious and most contested conservation approaches of the past three decades. Unlike conventional conservation, which typically aims to maintain existing ecosystems in a managed steady state, rewilding accepts ecological change and aims to restore the dynamic processes — predation, large herbivore grazing, natural hydrological cycles, natural fire regimes — that historically maintained ecosystem diversity and resilience. The premise is that ecosystems manage themselves more effectively when the full complement of native species and processes is present than when they are managed by human intervention in the absence of those processes.

The best-documented example of rewilding's effects is the reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone National Park in 1995, after an absence of seventy years. The wolves' predation on elk changed not only elk numbers but elk behaviour — elk avoided lingering in areas where they were vulnerable to predation, particularly valley bottoms and streamside vegetation. This behavioural change allowed the streamside vegetation to recover, which stabilised stream banks, changed water flow patterns, and produced changes in fish populations, bird populations, and beaver populations. These cascading effects — what ecologists call a trophic cascade — were not predicted in their extent and have become one of the most widely cited examples of how apex predators structure ecosystems.

The social dimensions of rewilding are as significant as the ecological ones. Large predators and herbivores do not recognise land ownership boundaries. Wolves that disperse from Yellowstone enter ranching country where they compete with livestock producers. Beavers whose dam-building floods agricultural land create conflict with farmers. The negotiation of these conflicts — finding compensation mechanisms, exploring livestock protection approaches, developing governance frameworks that give affected communities a genuine voice in rewilding decisions — is at least as important as the ecological science, and in many cases is the factor that determines whether rewilding projects succeed or fail.

title: Urban Nature: Finding Wildlife in Cities
body:
The assumption that cities are biological wastelands — places from which nature has been expelled to make room for the human enterprise — is wrong in ways that a closer look at any city confirms. Urban environments support extraordinary biodiversity: cities in the UK have been found to harbour more plant species per unit area than the surrounding countryside, because the variety of urban habitats — parks, railway embankments, canal towpaths, derelict land, garden walls, green roofs — creates a patchwork of ecological niches that no single type of managed agricultural land can match. The wildlife that has learned to exploit urban environments is not the diminished remnant of a richer fauna but, in many cases, thriving populations of species that have found cities advantageous.

Peregrine falcons — the fastest animals on Earth — now nest on city centre buildings across Europe and North America, where tall ledges mimic the cliff faces of their natural habitat and abundant feral pigeons provide a reliable food source. Urban foxes have established populations in virtually every British city and many continental European ones, exploiting the food resources of human settlements with remarkable intelligence and adaptability. Urban bat populations, sustained by the insect life that gathers around streetlights and rivers, have held up better than rural ones in many areas. The city, properly attended to, is a wildlife documentary happening in real time outside every window.

The practice of urban nature watching — attending deliberately to the non-human life sharing our cities — is both intrinsically rewarding and practically important. The species that are doing well in cities are doing well partly because urban habitats offer what more intensively managed rural landscapes have removed: structural diversity, food availability, and freedom from certain kinds of disturbance. Understanding what urban wildlife needs to thrive is directly applicable to rural conservation: the cities are, in this sense, inadvertent conservation experiments whose results are worth studying carefully.

title: The Sixth Mass Extinction: What It Means
body:
The Earth has experienced five mass extinction events in the past half billion years, during which more than seventy-five percent of existing species were lost in geologically brief periods: the Ordovician-Silurian, the Late Devonian, the Permian-Triassic (the 'Great Dying', which eliminated approximately ninety-six percent of marine species), the Triassic-Jurassic, and the Cretaceous-Paleogene (which ended the non-avian dinosaurs). Palaeobiologists studying current rates of species loss have concluded, with near unanimity, that we are in the early stages of a sixth such event — one driven not by asteroid impact or volcanic eruption but by human activity: habitat destruction, overexploitation, pollution, invasive species, and now climate change.

The numbers are stark. The current extinction rate is estimated at between one hundred and one thousand times the background rate — the rate that prevailed before human influence became significant. The IUCN Red List of threatened species includes over forty thousand species currently assessed as threatened with extinction. Vertebrate populations — mammals, birds, fish, reptiles, and amphibians — have declined by an average of almost seventy percent since 1970, according to the WWF Living Planet Index. These are not projections. They are measurements of what has already occurred within a single human lifetime.

The consequences of large-scale biodiversity loss extend well beyond the intrinsic value of the lost species. Ecosystems provide services — clean water, clean air, carbon storage, pollination, flood regulation, soil formation — that human economies depend on and that are produced by the interactions between the species that constitute those ecosystems. The loss of species disrupts these interactions in ways that are difficult to predict and impossible to fully compensate with technological substitutes. This is the strongest practical argument for biodiversity conservation: not sentiment but self-interest, correctly understood to extend beyond the immediate to the long-term and beyond the individual to the collective.

title: Soil: The Living Foundation We're Losing
body:
A teaspoon of healthy agricultural soil contains more microorganisms — bacteria, fungi, protozoa, nematodes — than there are people on Earth. This community of organisms is not incidental to soil's function. It is soil's function. The fertility of soil, its ability to provide plants with the nutrients they need in forms they can absorb, is primarily a product of biological activity — the decomposition of organic matter, the cycling of nitrogen, phosphorus, and other elements through the food web, the formation of stable soil aggregates that allow air and water to penetrate and roots to explore. Industrial agriculture, with its reliance on synthetic fertilisers, pesticides, and intensive tillage, has degraded this biological community across much of the world's agricultural land, and the consequences are becoming increasingly apparent.

Topsoil — the biologically rich upper layer in which most plant roots grow and most soil life is concentrated — is being lost through erosion at rates that dwarf its natural formation. It takes approximately a thousand years for natural processes to form a single centimetre of topsoil. Modern agricultural practices can lose that centimetre in a decade. The UN's Food and Agriculture Organisation estimated in 2015 that, without significant changes in agricultural practice, the world has approximately sixty years of topsoil left. This is a striking and sobering number, though the uncertainty around it is significant.

The alternatives to soil-degrading practices are well-established and increasingly widely adopted: reduced or no-till farming, cover cropping, diverse crop rotations, integration of livestock with arable farming, and organic matter additions all build rather than deplete soil health. The challenge is not knowledge but economics and scale: the incentive structures of contemporary agricultural markets do not reward the long-term investment in soil health that these practices represent. Changing those incentive structures — through agricultural policy, carbon markets, and supply chain requirements — is the policy challenge whose resolution will determine whether the world's soils can be restored before the consequences of their degradation become irreversible.

title: Britain's Lost Wildlife: And How It's Coming Back
body:
Britain has lost more of its wildlife than almost any other country in Europe — a consequence of the thoroughness of the agricultural transformation that began in the enclosures of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries and was accelerated by the intensification of farming in the post-war period. The numbers are dispiriting: ninety-seven percent of wildflower meadows lost since the 1930s, most ancient wetlands drained, hedgerows removed at a rate of hundreds of thousands of miles over the post-war decades, farmland bird populations collapsed by fifty percent since the 1970s. Britain, by biodiversity measures, is one of the most nature-depleted countries on Earth.

And yet there are genuine reasons for guarded optimism. The beaver — extinct in Britain for approximately four hundred years — has been reintroduced to a number of river catchments in England and Scotland, with results that have exceeded the expectations of even its most enthusiastic advocates. Within five years of their introduction to the River Otter in Devon, beavers had transformed the river ecosystem: their dams were slowing water flow, reducing flooding downstream, improving water quality, and creating wetland habitat that supported dramatic increases in invertebrate and amphibian populations. The ecosystem engineering capacity of beavers is one of the most powerful tools available for river habitat restoration, and the reintroductions underway represent a genuinely significant conservation advance.

The rewilding projects at Knepp Estate in West Sussex, where farming has been abandoned and native grazing animals allowed to shape the landscape, have demonstrated that the recovery of wildlife given the right conditions can be rapid and dramatic. Species that had not been recorded in the area for decades — including turtle doves, nightingales, purple emperor butterflies, and numerous rare beetle species — colonised the estate within years of the farming ceasing. Knepp has become both a proof of concept for large-scale rewilding and a tourist destination, demonstrating that the recovery of nature can generate economic value as well as ecological benefit.

title: The Science of Seasons: Why They Matter
body:
The seasons are the most fundamental rhythm of temperate life — the cycle of light and darkness, warmth and cold, growth and dormancy that has shaped the biology of every organism living above the subtropics and below them for hundreds of millions of years. Our own biology is profoundly seasonal: melatonin production is regulated by day length, immune function varies seasonally, metabolic rate shows seasonal variation, and the prevalence of both depression and mania varies with season in ways that are only partially explained by the psychological effects of reduced light. We are seasonal creatures who have constructed an artificial environment designed to deny seasonality, and the consequences of this denial are only beginning to be studied.

The phenology of seasonal events — the timing of bud burst, first butterfly, first cuckoo call, peak blossom — is among the most sensitive indicators of climate change available to ecologists. The careful records kept by naturalists over centuries, and the systematic phenological networks established more recently, show that spring events are arriving earlier across most of the temperate zone, with significant ecological consequences. Species whose seasonal timing is constrained by factors other than temperature — day length, for instance, which is not changing — are falling out of synchrony with species whose timing has shifted in response to warming. The consequence is what ecologists call phenological mismatch: the peak abundance of caterpillars arriving before the peak period when great tit chicks need them, for instance, reducing breeding success.

The practice of attending to seasonal change — keeping a nature diary, noting the first swallow, the first bluebell, the date of the first frost — is both intrinsically rewarding and, in aggregate, scientifically valuable. The citizen science platforms that aggregate these observations from thousands of contributors are producing datasets of a breadth and continuity that no professional research programme could match. Attending to the seasons is not nostalgia or sentiment. It is a form of literacy that our species has carried for hundreds of thousands of years and that the modern world has systematically eroded. Recovering it is both personally enriching and ecologically important.

title: Plastic Pollution: What We Know and What We Can Do
body:
Plastic production has increased from approximately two million tonnes per year in 1950 to over four hundred million tonnes per year today, with roughly forty percent of all plastic produced used for packaging that is discarded almost immediately. The proportion of this plastic that is collected and recycled is small — approximately nine percent globally, as of the most recent assessments — with the remainder going to landfill, incineration, or unmanaged disposal. The fraction that enters the ocean is estimated at eight to twelve million tonnes per year, adding to the accumulation already present in ocean gyres, on shorelines, and in ocean sediments.

The environmental consequences of plastic pollution are serious and increasingly well-documented. Seabirds, marine mammals, and sea turtles ingest plastic or become entangled in plastic debris, with significant mortality consequences. The breakdown of plastic into microplastics — particles under five millimetres — and nanoplastics creates a more diffuse but potentially more pervasive hazard: these particles are now found in every compartment of the environment, including the deep ocean sediments, polar ice, mountain air, human blood, placental tissue, and the lungs of people who have never been near the ocean. The biological consequences of chronic microplastic exposure are under active investigation, with preliminary findings suggesting effects on endocrine function, immune response, and development.

The individual actions available — refusing single-use plastics, choosing products with minimal or recyclable packaging, supporting extended producer responsibility legislation — are genuinely useful but insufficient at the scale of the problem. The systemic changes required are policy changes: regulation of plastic production, mandatory recycled content in new products, extended producer responsibility that places the cost of disposal on manufacturers, and investment in waste management infrastructure in the countries where most plastic leakage into the environment currently occurs. The gap between what individuals can do and what is actually needed is one of the most important facts about the plastic problem, and acknowledging it honestly is the beginning of productive engagement with it.

title: Ancient Forests: What Makes a Wood Old
body:
Ancient woodland — woodland that has been continuously wooded since at least 1600 in England and Wales, 1750 in Scotland — is among the most ecologically valuable habitat in Britain and among the most irreplaceable. The threshold dates are not biologically meaningful; they reflect the availability of historical maps from which continuity can be verified. What is biologically meaningful is the time that woodland continuity provides for the accumulation of the ecological complexity that makes ancient woodland distinctive: the deep, undisturbed soil horizons with their community of specialist invertebrates and fungi, the standing and fallen dead wood in multiple stages of decay, the veteran trees with their extraordinary structural complexity, and the community of vascular plants — including the ancient woodland indicator species — whose colonisation rates are so slow that their presence is itself a record of historical continuity.

Ancient woodland indicator plants — species like wood anemone, bluebell, wild garlic, yellow archangel, herb Paris, wood sorrel — colonise new woodland extremely slowly, moving outward from existing populations at rates measurable in metres per century rather than per decade. Their presence in a woodland is therefore reliable evidence of long continuity, and their community composition provides a richer ecological signal than any other easily surveyed feature. The ancient woodland surveys conducted using indicator species have identified approximately 340,000 hectares of ancient woodland in England — representing just 2.5% of England's total land area.

The threats to ancient woodland remain serious despite legal protections. Development pressure — road schemes, housing, industrial developments — continues to destroy irreplaceable ancient woodland, often with planning consents that include mitigation conditions (translocation of soil, transplanting of indicator plants) whose ecological effectiveness is, to put it charitably, unproven. The practical truth is that ancient woodland cannot be recreated on a human time horizon. Once lost, it is lost. This makes its protection not merely a conservation priority but a statement about what relationship with time and with the past we want to maintain in our planning for the future.
